pattern: Map each word to its concatenated dot-dash encoding and count how many distinct encodings result by inserting them into a set.
hints: Translate every letter to its fixed dot-dash string and glue them together to get one code per word.
Two different words can produce the same code, so equality is decided on the encoded string, not the original word.
Collect every word's encoding into a set and return the set's size.

edge_inputs.description: four words collapsing to two distinct codes
different letters that encode to the same string still count once
every single letter of the alphabet, all distinct
longest words sharing a prefix but differing at the end

stress.description: many random lowercase words so collisions among encodings appear
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, size))
    words = []
    for _ in range(n):
        length = rng.randint(1, 12)
        words.append(''.join(rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(length)))
    return [words]
stress.note: random short words exercise the set-dedup path
